title: How Psychedelics Affect Time Perception
body:
How Psychedelics Affect Time Perception

1. A Moment Out of Time: My First Encounter with Psychedelic Time

Time, as we measure it, is one of those things we rarely question. We glance at clocks, count seconds, and keep busy schedules, trusting that time flows evenly and predictably. But then, there was that first time I took a psychedelic mushroom, a moment that didn’t just bend my mind but bent time itself. I remember lying on the grass, the sky above me shifting in colors I couldn’t name, and my sense of time completely vanishing. Minutes stretched into what felt like eternities, yet paradoxically, hours disappeared before I knew it. I had stepped outside of time’s usual path, and that experience stuck with me.

What happens when time slips out of our control? How does a substance alter something so fundamental to our reality? From ancient shamans to modern therapists, the distortion of time is one of the most reported and intriguing effects of psychedelics. It’s not just an odd side effect but often a doorway into deep psychological insights, healing, and altered states of consciousness. In my years writing about and working with psychedelic therapy, I’ve seen time distortion as a kind of psychological reset button, something that breaks the grip of our rigid, clock-driven lives.

But it’s not always gentle or pleasant. The warping of time can confuse, frighten, or leave us grappling with our sense of identity. It raises questions about how our brain constructs time in the first place and what happens when that construction temporarily falls apart. This article is an exploration of that strange, slippery phenomenon. We’ll look at what science says about how psychedelics affect our perception of time, the research grounding those findings, and most importantly, how understanding time distortion can deepen your psychedelic experience and your daily life.

If you’ve ever felt like time slowed down to a crawl during a psychedelic session or sped up while you were reflecting afterward, you’re not alone. This experience touches everyone differently, yet it’s one of the most universal features of psychedelics. And understanding it can open doors to new ways of healing and being present. I hope you’ll join me on this journey through time - warped, stretched, and transformed by the quiet power of psychedelics.

2. The Science of Time Perception and Psychedelic States

To understand how psychedelics affect our experience of time, we first need to grasp how the brain normally processes time. Our sense of time isn’t like a clock in the brain ticking away uniformly. It’s a complex, distributed process involving several brain regions that encode duration, sequence, and temporal order. The basal ganglia, cerebellum, prefrontal cortex, and parietal lobes work together to create our subjective experience of time’s flow. The neurotransmitter dopamine also plays a huge role, especially in interval timing.

When you take a psychedelic, such as psilocybin or LSD, you’re primarily interacting with the serotonin 2A (5-HT2A) receptors, particularly in the neocortex. These substances scramble the usual communication patterns in the brain, especially within the default mode network (DMN), a set of regions associated with self-referential thinking and temporal continuity. Robin Carhart-Harris and his team have shown how psychedelics reduce activity and connectivity in the DMN. This “loosening” of the brain’s hierarchical organization can disrupt how we construct a linear sense of time.

Under psychedelics, the brain enters a state of increased entropy - a kind of chaotic, less predictable firing pattern. Instead of moving forward in a neat, ordered sequence, neural activity feels more fluid and less constrained. This can lead to time dilation, where moments seem to stretch endlessly, or time contraction, when hours fly by in what feels like minutes. The phenomenon feels like slipping out of the usual narrative that connects past, present, and future into a continuous thread.

Additionally, psychedelics often alter our body awareness, which is tightly linked to how we estimate time. The internal bodily signals that help us gauge the passage of time, called interoceptive cues, become distorted. You might feel your heartbeat or breath slowing down or speeding up, or lose track of physical sensations altogether, further muddling your sense of temporal flow.

In short, psychedelics affect multiple layers of how the brain processes time - from sensory input to higher-level cognitive integration. When those layers uncouple, time perception can become malleable, surprising, and sometimes disorienting. This is a fascinating area where subjective experience intersects with neuroscience, offering clues about how the mind constructs reality itself.

3. What the Research Shows: Studies on Psychedelics and Time

There’s a growing body of scientific research focused on how psychedelics influence time perception, and some of it has been truly illuminating. One landmark study by Wittmann et al. in 2007 examined psilocybin’s effects on subjective time perception using a time estimation task. Volunteers under psilocybin tended to overestimate the duration of intervals, sometimes by a factor of two or more, showing clear time dilation. The researchers concluded this was tied to altered processing in brain areas involved in timing and temporal judgment.

More recently, Carhart-Harris and colleagues published data highlighting reduced DMN integrity alongside increased neural entropy under psilocybin, correlating with subjective reports of expanded time and timelessness. These findings suggest that the breakdown of the brain’s default mode network disrupts the usual temporal structure that gives time its linear shape.

Stanislav Grof, one of the pioneers of psychedelic therapy, emphasized time distortion as a core aspect of extended psychedelic states, describing “timelessness” or “eternity” experiences that can feel both overwhelming and liberating. His observations, though primarily clinical and anecdotal, align with what modern neuroimaging studies have begun to confirm.

Another study by Roseman et al. (2018) looked at psilocybin’s effects on the brain and found that altered time perception was linked to changes in the brain’s sensory processing. This ties into the way psychedelics create a hyperconnected brain state where sensory and cognitive processes blend, blurring the boundaries of time as a distinct dimension.

Timothy Leary and Richard Alpert (later Ram Dass) documented early psychedelic research in the 1960s, noting how users reported “time loops” and “eternal now” feelings. Modern researchers have built on this foundation with brain imaging tools like fMRI and EEG, giving more concrete evidence to those subjective reports.

For those interested in going deeper, Michael Pollan’s How to Change Your Mind offers excellent narrative accounts and interview excerpts explaining these altered temporal states in an accessible way. It’s a must-read if you want to understand how the brain’s timekeeping breaks down under psychedelics and what that means for healing and consciousness.

4. Applying the Experience: Using Time Distortion to Your Advantage

At first glance, time distortion might seem like a mere curiosity or something to brace for. But it can actually be an incredibly useful aspect of a psychedelic journey. For one, it creates space - mental and emotional space - that allows for insights to arise without the pressure of chronological urgency. When time feels infinite or irrelevant, you can fully immerse yourself in sensations, memories, or emotions that normally get pushed aside in the rush of daily life.

One practical step I recommend is setting an intention around time before your session. For example, deciding that you want to explore your relationship with time itself - perhaps unresolved trauma around deadlines, aging, or impatience. By focusing on time as a theme, you can bring attention to how your mind resists or surrenders to the present moment’s flow. During the session, notice how time stretches or contracts and see what feelings come up.

Journaling is another powerful tool, especially after the experience. Recording your thoughts and perceptions about time can reveal patterns and shifts. If you’re looking for a dedicated place to do this, a Journaling Notebook Leather Bound is a solid choice. Writing about these distortions often uncovers deeper insights about how your mind is wired around control and surrender.

In therapy or integration circles, time distortion can help loosen rigid narratives that trap us in painful loops. For instance, trauma survivors frequently relive past events as if stuck in a time loop, unable to move forward. Psychedelics’ capacity to shift time perception can help disrupt those loops, offering a new vantage point to process and integrate trauma memories. This idea echoes Bessel van der Kolk’s work in The Body Keeps the Score, where trauma shapes the body’s and mind’s sense of time and safety.

So, rather than fearing time distortion, you can frame it as a tool for exploration. By relaxing your grip on clock time, you might find stuck emotions unwinding, creative insights emerging, or a deeper connection to the present moment growing. This approach requires patience and curiosity but can transform what might feel like confusion into clarity.

5. Navigating Common Challenges with Psychedelic Time

Not every encounter with psychedelic time is smooth. For many, the warping of time can cause anxiety, panic, or even a sense of losing oneself entirely. Moments that feel like they stretch on forever can become unbearable, especially if you’re struggling with difficult emotions or memories. Conversely, the rapid passage of time might leave you feeling disoriented or like you missed important parts of your journey.

One common challenge is the feeling of being stuck in an endless loop or “time loop.” This can make the experience feel like a trap and intensify feelings of dread. If this happens, grounding techniques can help. Focus on your breath, notice physical sensations in your body, or remind yourself that the experience is temporary. Bringing gentle awareness back to your body can re-anchor you in the present moment and reduce the overwhelming sense of timelessness.

Another difficulty arises when your internal clock dramatically shifts after the session. You might feel as though days or weeks have passed in moments, or that time is rushing too fast outside the psychedelic experience. This post-psychedelic time distortion can affect sleep, motivation, and emotional regulation. Planning for integration support, whether through therapy, peer groups, or self-care routines, is important.

Many people find that journaling their experiences, even difficult ones, helps them process these distortions. Writing down what you felt and thought during moments of time stretching or collapsing can create a container for those experiences. Over time, this practice can reduce anxiety about losing control and build a healthier relationship with altered time perception.

Lastly, preparation matters. If you’re nervous about time distortion, talk about these fears with a trusted sitter or therapist before your session. Sometimes knowing what to expect can relieve the pressure and help you lean into the experience rather than resist it. Remember, the brain will usually recalibrate within hours to days after the session, so even the most unsettling distortions fade.

6. Integration and the Long View: Living with Changed Time Perception

After the psychedelic effects wear off, the shifts in how you perceive time may linger subtly or clearly. I’ve often seen clients report a newfound patience or slower pace in their daily lives, a kind of recalibration that makes the frantic rush of normal life seem absurd. This might sound like a blessing, but it also requires integration - the deliberate work of taking psychedelic insights and making them part of your ongoing life.

One way to integrate this altered time perception is by practicing mindfulness and presence. When you learn to sense time as less rigid, you can start to prioritize moments of stillness and awareness rather than constantly chasing productivity. This shift aligns with what Grof called “expanded consciousness,” where time loses its tight grip and you live more fully in each moment’s fullness.

Another integration practice involves reflecting on how your relationship with time affects your mental health. For instance, many anxiety disorders involve a kind of future-focused worry, where the mind races ahead to what might go wrong. Psychedelic experiences can give you a glimpse beyond that anxious timeline, a chance to revisit the present. Incorporating daily rituals that honor this perspective - like journaling in a leather-bound notebook or gentle breathwork - can anchor these lessons.

At the same time, be realistic. Our society runs on clock time, deadlines, and schedules. Finding a balance between the psychedelic experience’s fluid time and the everyday world’s demands is key. This doesn’t mean losing your psychedelic insights but rather letting them soften your approach to time. You might slow down your morning routine, add pauses during the day, or create “time outs” to connect with your body and breath - all ways to stretch the container of your usual time.

Finally, staying open to the ongoing evolution of your time perception can be healing. Psychedelic journeys aren’t one-off events but part of a long conversation with yourself and the world. Over time, your sense of time may become less linear and rigid, allowing more room for creativity, healing, and presence. Books like The Body Keeps the Score remind us how trauma and time are intertwined, and how rewiring these pathways takes patience and care.

7. Sitting with Time: A Final Thought

Time is something we all live inside, yet it’s one of the most mysterious aspects of our experience. Psychedelics amplify that mystery, pushing us to confront time not as a fixed line but as a flowing, shifting dimension. After many years of working with and writing about psychedelics, I’ve come to see time distortion not as a problem to fix but as a doorway to a different understanding of existence.

Imagine if time isn’t just a relentless march forward but a field you can wander, sometimes speeding up, other times slowing down, occasionally standing still. Psychedelic time can open us to this possibility, revealing that our minds are the architects of the temporal world we inhabit. This awareness can be both humbling and liberating.

Yet, beyond the neuroscience and research, beyond the altered states, there is a simple, human urge to hold on to something steady. We crave stability, meaning, and connection. Psychedelic time challenges these cravings, inviting us to loosen our hold and trust the unfolding moment, no matter how strange or unfamiliar. It’s a practice, a skill to be developed - much like any deep healing or growth.

So when you next find yourself wondering where the minutes went or feeling like time stretches like taffy, try sitting with that sensation. Don’t rush to label it good or bad, real or unreal. Instead, let it ask its own questions. What does time feel like when you aren’t trying to hold onto it? How might that loosened grip change how you see yourself and the world?

In the end, psychedelics teach us, among many things, that time is a living, breathing part of the human experience - not just something to be measured, but something to be felt, explored, and respected. And that, in itself, is a quiet kind of medicine.
excerpt: Time, as we commonly perceive it, is an elaborate fiction, a consensus hallucination we collectively agree upon, but beneath the surface of clocks and calendars, there lies a deeper, more fluid reality. Psychedelics invite us to dive into this primordial soup of experience, dramatically altering our subjective temporality and challenging our conventional understanding of time.
pullQuote: The ability of psychedelics to alter time perception holds significant implications for therapeutic applications, particularly in the treatment of conditions where individuals are trapped in repetitive thought patterns or the persistent echoes of past traumas.
heroPrompt: A luminous, warm, and abstract representation of time flowing and stretching, with gentle, swirling patterns of light and color that evoke a sense of expansion and timelessness. No clocks or literal timekeeping devices. Focus on soft, healing light.
heroAlt: Abstract illustration of flowing light and color, representing the malleable nature of time and consciousness, with warm, soft hues suggesting healing and expansion.

faqs.question: How do psychedelics make time feel different?
faqs.answer: Psychedelics like psilocybin and LSD can disrupt the brain's default mode network, which is involved in our sense of linear time and self-narrative. This can lead to experiences where minutes feel like hours, or entire lifetimes seem to unfold in moments, effectively bending and stretching subjective temporal perception.

faqs.question: Can altered time perception from psychedelics have lasting effects?
faqs.answer: While the acute effects of altered time perception fade, the insights and new perspectives gained can have lasting impacts. Many individuals report a sustained increase in mindfulness and present-moment awareness, leading to a less rigid and more spacious relationship with time in their daily lives, helping to reduce anxiety about the past or future.
